summary:
The primary lymphatic drainage pathway varies across regions; while most areas drain centrally, a notable portion—specifically the posterior part—drains directly into deeper neck structures without passing through superficial tissues first. This highlights regional variations crucial for understanding comprehensive draining mechanisms. To address specific anatomical queries accurately requires precise localization and consideration of these distinctions. ```plaintext
The main lymph from the tongue can vary significantly based on location, but notably, much of it drains centrally whereas the posterior one-third flows directly into deep cervical lymph nodes. Understanding such region-specific differences is essential for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.
```
